text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tones}} is defined as: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tones
Description: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tones' es: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Erhua (儿化)}} is defined as: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Erhua (儿化)
Description: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Erhua (儿化)' es: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs/Triphthongs}} is defined as: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Diphthongs/Triphthongs' es: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration Contrast}} is defined as: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Aspiration Contrast
Description: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Aspiration Contrast' es: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Three Series}} is defined as: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: The Three Series
Description: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'The Three Series' es: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Retroflex}} is defined as: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Retroflex
Description: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Retroflex' es: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Alveolo-palatal}} is defined as: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Alveolo-palatal' es: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Erhua (Retroflexion - 儿化)}} is defined as: Adding a retroflexed 'r' sound to the end of syllables, highly common in Beijing and Northern dialects. It modifies the main vowel:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:

Description: Adding a retroflexed 'r' sound to the end of syllables, highly common in Beijing and Northern dialects. It modifies the main vowel:

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)}} is defined as: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)
Description: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)' es: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Bu Sandhi}} is defined as: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Bu Sandhi
Description: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Bu Sandhi' es: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Yi Sandhi}} is defined as: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Yi Sandhi
Description: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Yi Sandhi' es: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Half 3rd Tone Rule}} is defined as: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Half 3rd Tone Rule
Description: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Half 3rd Tone Rule' es: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Colloquial Syllable Contraction}} is defined as: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Colloquial Syllable Contraction
Description: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Colloquial Syllable Contraction' es: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tonal Identity}} is defined as: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tonal Identity
Description: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tonal Identity' es: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Neutral Tone}} is defined as: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Neutral Tone
Description: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Neutral Tone' es: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.

text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Zhiao" Shortcut}}? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
usage:
Tip: The "Zhiao" Shortcut
In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The "Zhiao" Shortcut'? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!

text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Half 3rd Tone Rule}}? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
usage:
Tip: The Half 3rd Tone Rule
To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The Half 3rd Tone Rule'? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
